race.name: RBC GranFondo Whistler
race.tagline: 122 km from Vancouver to Whistler on a fully closed Sea-to-Sky Highway. North America's largest gran fondo — 5,000+ riders crossing the Lions Gate Bridge at sunrise and climbing through the Coast Mountains on the one day a year this highway belongs to bikes.

vitals.location: Vancouver to Whistler, British Columbia, Canada
vitals.start_time: Saturday 7:00 AM (GranFondo) / 6:00 AM (Forte) from Stanley Park, Vancouver
vitals.registration: Online via rbcgranfondo.com. CAD $200-300 (~$145-220 USD) depending on distance category and registration tier. Early-bird pricing available. Under-19 discounted at $50.
vitals.prize_purse: None. Timed competitive categories with awards, but primarily a mass-participation gran fondo.
vitals.aid_stations: 4 official aid stations spaced approximately 25 km apart starting at Britannia Beach (km 51). Water, electrolytes, energy food, fruit. On-course entertainment and medical support throughout.
vitals.cutoff_time: Approximately 8.5 hours for the GranFondo. Full course support with SAG vehicles and sweeper.
distance_options: 152 km Forte (~95 mi, 3,100m / 10,170 ft climbing — adds Cypress Mountain climb)
122 km GranFondo (~76 mi, 2,300m / 7,546 ft climbing — the classic route)
55 km Medio (~34 mi, 835m / 2,740 ft climbing — Whistler-based, e-bike friendly)

climate.primary: Pacific Northwest late summer — mild, variable, coastal-to-alpine transition
climate.description: Early September in the Sea-to-Sky corridor is typically the tail end of British Columbia's dry season. Vancouver starts cool at 50-60F (10-16C) in the early morning, warming to 60-75F (16-24C) through the day. Whistler sits higher and cooler. The route transitions from coastal to alpine climate across 122 km. Rain is possible — this is the Pacific Northwest — but September is statistically one of the driest months. Morning fog along Howe Sound burns off. The second half from Squamish to Whistler can bring afternoon headwinds and heat radiating off the highway.
challenges: Early morning start in cool Vancouver (50-55F) with potential fog on Howe Sound
Temperature differential between sea-level start and mountain finish
Possible afternoon headwinds in the Squamish-to-Whistler corridor
Rain is always possible in the Pacific Northwest — even in September's dry window
Heat radiation off highway pavement in the second half on warm days

terrain.primary: Fully paved Sea-to-Sky Highway — coastal then mountain climbing
terrain.surface: Smooth paved highway throughout. The Sea-to-Sky Highway was significantly upgraded for the 2010 Winter Olympics, making it one of the best-maintained stretches of road in British Columbia. The first half is coastal and rolling along Howe Sound with some punchy climbs at Furry Creek and Britannia Beach. The second half from Squamish to Whistler is a sustained mountain ascent through the Coast Range. All roads fully closed to traffic for the event — a dedicated car-free lane the entire way.
features: Lions Gate Bridge crossing at sunrise — iconic Vancouver landmark
Howe Sound coastal riding with ocean and mountain views
Sea-to-Sky Highway — fully closed to traffic, Olympic-upgraded surface
Furry Creek and Britannia Beach punch climbs
Sustained Squamish-to-Whistler mountain ascent through the Coast Range
Whistler Village finish — resort town atmosphere

gravel_god_rating.tier_note: North America's largest and most awarded gran fondo. The Sea-to-Sky Highway on closed roads is a bucket-list cycling experience. T2 by score (61). Prestige=3 — no override applies.
gravel_god_rating.score_note: Experience=5 (crossing Lions Gate Bridge at sunrise then riding a closed highway through the Coast Mountains alongside Howe Sound is genuinely world-class scenery). Race_quality=4 (RBC title sponsorship, award-winning organization, fully closed highway — premium production). Adventure=4 (the Sea-to-Sky corridor is one of North America's great scenic drives, and you get it car-free). Technicality=1 and altitude=1 keep the score honest — this is smooth highway with negligible altitude.

logistics.explanation: Vancouver International Airport (YVR) is a major international hub with direct flights from across North America, Europe, and Asia-Pacific. The start line at Stanley Park is in downtown Vancouver — trivially accessible. The finish in Whistler Village has abundant accommodation and a resort infrastructure built for tourists. Event logistics are polished: bus transport from Whistler back to Vancouver is included, packet pickup at a consumer expo, and the start/finish flow is refined over 15+ years. The only friction is that it's a point-to-point — you need to manage accommodation in both cities or rely on the return shuttle.

length.explanation: 122 km (76 miles) with 2,300 meters of climbing is a properly demanding day on the bike. Most riders finish in 3.5-8.5 hours depending on fitness. It's not an ultra-distance event, but the climbing makes it substantially harder than a flat century. The Forte option at 152 km with 3,100m of climbing pushes into serious endurance territory. For the mass-participation target audience, 122 km on the Sea-to-Sky is exactly the right distance — long enough to be an achievement, short enough that anyone who trains can finish.

technicality.explanation: This is a closed highway. Smooth pavement, wide lanes, zero traffic. The descents from Furry Creek are manageable for anyone who can hold a handlebar. There are some curves along the Howe Sound coastal sections, but the road is wide and well-sighted. If you can ride a road bike in a straight line and take a corner at 40 km/h, you have all the technical skill this event requires. The Sea-to-Sky was rebuilt for the Olympics — the road surface is impeccable.

elevation.explanation: 2,300 meters (7,546 feet) of climbing is meaningful. The profile is deceptive — the first half along Howe Sound is rolling with some punch climbs (Furry Creek at 5.8% average), but the real work starts after Squamish. From there it's a sustained 60+ km grind uphill into the Coast Mountains. Most of the 2,300m happens in the second half. It's not Alpine switchbacks, but the accumulated vertical from sea level to Whistler Village (670m elevation) will grind down undertrained legs. Enough climbing to separate the prepared from the ambitious.

climate.explanation: September in the Sea-to-Sky corridor is as good as Pacific Northwest weather gets — but that's a relative statement. Temperatures are comfortable (55-75F), rain probability is low but never zero, and the conditions are generally benign for cycling. The temperature drop from sea level to Whistler is noticeable but not extreme. The potential afternoon headwind in the Squamish-to-Whistler valley is annoying, not dangerous. Climate is simply not a significant challenge factor here — it's rarely too hot, too cold, or too wet.

altitude.explanation: You start at sea level in Vancouver and finish at 670 meters (2,200 feet) in Whistler Village. The highest point on the route is roughly 700 meters. Altitude is completely irrelevant. Nobody is gasping for air. This is a coastal-to-moderate-elevation ride. The elevation gain is from climbing, not from altitude.

adventure.explanation: The Sea-to-Sky Highway is one of North America's most spectacular drives, and riding it car-free on a bicycle is a genuinely rare experience. Lions Gate Bridge at sunrise. Howe Sound's fjord-like waters with mountains plunging to the ocean. The Stawamus Chief granite monolith looming over Squamish. The Coast Mountains closing in as you climb toward Whistler. This is world-class scenery delivered on a silver platter of closed highway. It's not backcountry adventure — it's civilized, infrastructure-heavy, fully supported — but the landscape does the heavy lifting, and it lifts hard.

prestige.explanation: North America's largest gran fondo. Winner of multiple 'Best Gran Fondo in North America' awards (2017, 2018, 2019, 2023, 2024). RBC title sponsorship gives it corporate legitimacy. Riders from 48 countries. But let's be honest — gran fondos are not road races. This isn't the Tour de France, it's not even a major professional race. It's a mass-participation event with tremendous production value and a loyal following. Within the gran fondo world, it's near the top. In global cycling prestige, it's a well-known participatory event. Prestige 3 is fair — respected, popular, not legendary.

race_quality.explanation: This is where the RBC money shows. Fully closed highway — not just cones and a painted lane, but actual road closure. Four well-stocked aid stations. On-course entertainment. Premium swag bag and finisher medal. Consumer expo at packet pickup. Finish line festival in Whistler Village with beer, wine, and hot food. Bus transport back to Vancouver included. Medical support throughout. 15+ years of organizational refinement. This is one of the most professionally produced cycling events in North America. The only reason it's not a 5 is that it's a gran fondo, not a race — there's no prize purse, no elite field management, no real competitive infrastructure.

experience.explanation: This is the event's defining score. You start in Stanley Park at dawn. You cross the Lions Gate Bridge with 5,000 other riders as the sun comes up over the North Shore mountains. You ride along Howe Sound — a coastal fjord with mountains dropping into turquoise water — on a highway that is completely, beautifully, impossibly car-free. You climb through the Coast Mountains. You finish in Whistler Village — a world-famous resort town that welcomes you with a party. The scenery alone would justify a 4. The closed-highway experience — riding where cars normally dominate, in perfect silence except for the hum of 5,000 sets of tires — that pushes it to 5. This is a day you remember.

community.explanation: 5,000+ riders from 48 countries creates a diverse, enthusiastic, genuinely warm community. The gran fondo format encourages camaraderie over competition. Riders draft together, encourage each other on the climbs, celebrate at the finish. The expanded programming — shakeout rides, post-event yoga, expo — builds connection beyond the ride itself. The loyalty rewards program and return-rider culture means many participants know each other year over year. The cycling community in Vancouver and Whistler is strong and welcoming. It's not a small, tight-knit tribe — it's a large, generous one.

field_depth.explanation: Let's be clear: this is a mass-participation gran fondo, not a professional road race. There are timed competitive categories, and the front of the field includes strong amateur racers and local pros. But the vast majority of riders are recreational cyclists riding for the experience, not the podium. If you want to race, the competitive categories provide a framework, but you're racing within a giant participatory event. The depth is in the breadth of participation, not the competitive pedigree. Don't come here expecting a race. Come here expecting the ride of your life.

value.explanation: CAD $200-300 (~$145-220 USD) for a fully closed highway, four aid stations, premium swag, finisher medal, return bus transport, and a finish-line festival is honestly fair value. You're paying for the road closure alone — the logistics of shutting down a major provincial highway for a day are enormous. The RBC sponsorship presumably offsets some costs. It's not cheap, but for what you get — an experience that simply cannot be replicated on any other day of the year — the price is justified. Not a bargain, not a ripoff.

expenses.explanation: Vancouver and Whistler are two of the most expensive cities in Canada. Hotels in Whistler Village during GranFondo weekend start at CAD $250-400/night and climb steeply. Vancouver accommodation is CAD $200-350/night. You need at least two nights — one pre-race in Vancouver, one post-race in Whistler (or a shuttle back). Food in both cities is expensive by North American standards. Flights to YVR are reasonable from most North American cities but add up from overseas. Budget CAD $800-1,500 for a weekend trip beyond the entry fee. This is a premium destination event in a premium destination.

biased_opinion.summary: There is one day a year when the Sea-to-Sky Highway — British Columbia's most famous road, the corridor that connects Vancouver's glass towers to Whistler's snow-capped peaks — belongs entirely to bicycles. That day is GranFondo day. You roll out of Stanley Park at dawn. You cross the Lions Gate Bridge with 5,000 riders and the sun rising over the North Shore. You ride along Howe Sound's fjord-blue water with mountains falling into the ocean on both sides. You climb through the Coast Range on pavement so smooth and so empty of cars that you forget this is normally a highway. You finish in Whistler Village, where a beer and a party and the disbelief that you just did that are all waiting. The RBC GranFondo Whistler is not a hard-man's race. It's not technical, it's not remote, and it won't destroy you. But it will give you 122 km of closed-highway riding through some of the most spectacular scenery in North America, and that experience — the sheer, rare luxury of a car-free highway through the mountains — is something you simply cannot get any other way.
strengths: Fully closed Sea-to-Sky Highway: the one day a year this iconic road belongs to bikes, not cars
World-class scenery: Lions Gate Bridge, Howe Sound, Coast Mountains, Whistler Village
Premium organization: 15+ years of refinement, RBC sponsorship, award-winning production
Accessible challenge: 2,300m of climbing is demanding but achievable for trained recreational riders
Whistler finish: ending at a world-class resort village makes the post-ride experience memorable
weaknesses: Not a race: competitive depth is limited — this is a participatory event with timed categories
Expensive destination: Vancouver and Whistler hotel costs make this a premium-budget weekend
Technicality is zero: if you want anything beyond smooth highway, look elsewhere
Weather uncertainty: the Pacific Northwest can always deliver rain, even in September
Point-to-point logistics: you need accommodation strategy for two cities 122 km apart
biased_opinion.bottom_line: 5,000 riders on a closed highway through the Coast Mountains. The scenery does the talking, the organization does the heavy lifting, and you do the climbing.

final_verdict.one_liner: 122 km on a closed highway from Vancouver to Whistler. The Sea-to-Sky belongs to bikes for one day — and it's magnificent.
final_verdict.should_you_race: Yes if you want one of North America's premier mass-participation cycling experiences with world-class scenery and bulletproof organization. Yes if riding a closed highway through the Coast Mountains sounds like your idea of paradise. Skip if you want a competitive road race, technical terrain, or a budget-friendly weekend.
final_verdict.alternatives: If you want a Canadian gran fondo with more competition: try the Banff Gran Fondo. If you want Sea-to-Sky riding without the event: ride Highway 99 any summer day (with traffic). If you want a similar-scale European gran fondo: try the Maratona dles Dolomites.

logistics.airport: Vancouver International Airport (YVR) — major international hub with direct flights from across North America, Europe, and Asia-Pacific. Located approximately 30 minutes from downtown Vancouver and the Stanley Park start area.
logistics.lodging_strategy: Point-to-point complicates accommodation. Most riders stay in Vancouver the night before (downtown hotels near Stanley Park: CAD $200-350/night) and either bus back to Vancouver post-race or stay in Whistler Saturday night (CAD $250-400/night at resort pricing). Book Whistler accommodation months in advance — event weekend books out fast. Tourism Whistler is the official accommodation partner. Budget-conscious riders can stay in Squamish (halfway point, more affordable) and shuttle to the start. Some riders book two nights in Whistler and take the shuttle to Vancouver for the start.
logistics.food: Vancouver and Whistler are both excellent food cities. Pre-race carb loading in Vancouver: countless pasta, sushi, and West Coast restaurants downtown. Post-race in Whistler Village: the finish festival includes beer, wine, and hot food. Whistler's restaurant scene ranges from casual pub fare to fine dining. Coffee culture is strong in both cities — flat whites and pour-overs are the Pacific Northwest way.
logistics.packet_pickup: Consumer expo and packet pickup typically held Friday in Vancouver. Event includes premium swag bag, rider bib, and finisher medal. Race briefing and expo with cycling brand vendors.
logistics.parking: If driving to the start, limited parking near Stanley Park — arrive very early or use event-designated parking with shuttle service. Many riders use ride-share or transit to the start. Return to Vancouver from Whistler is via included bus transport.

history.founder: GranFondo Canada / RBC (Royal Bank of Canada) as title sponsor
history.origin_story: Born in the euphoria following the 2010 Vancouver Winter Olympics, the GranFondo Whistler was conceived to put the Sea-to-Sky Highway — the corridor that had carried the world to the Whistler Olympic venues — back in the spotlight. The idea was audacious: close one of British Columbia's most critical transportation arteries for an entire day and let thousands of cyclists ride it car-free. RBC signed on as title sponsor early, lending the event corporate credibility and financial muscle. The first edition drew enthusiastic crowds, and the event quickly grew into North America's largest gran fondo. The Sea-to-Sky Highway's Olympic-era upgrades — widened lanes, improved surface, new safety features — made it an ideal cycling venue. What started as a post-Olympic celebration became an annual institution.
notable_moments: 2010: Inaugural event launched following the Vancouver Winter Olympics, leveraging the upgraded Sea-to-Sky Highway
RBC (Royal Bank of Canada) signs on as title sponsor, establishing corporate-backed cycling tradition
Multiple 'Best Gran Fondo in North America' awards (2017, 2018, 2019, 2023, 2024)
2022: Event returns post-COVID with renewed energy and strong participation
2024: Record-breaking 15th anniversary — 5,245 registered riders from 48 countries, 14,000+ riders and spectators
2024: First paracyclist completes the 152 km Forte distance
Forte distance category added — 152 km with Cypress Mountain climb before joining the GranFondo route
Generates over CAD $5 million annually for British Columbia's economy
history.reputation: North America's largest and most awarded gran fondo. The gold standard for mass-participation cycling events on the continent.

course_description.character: A point-to-point journey from sea level to the mountains along one of North America's most scenic highways. The course starts in Vancouver's Stanley Park, crosses the Lions Gate Bridge, and follows the Sea-to-Sky Highway north along Howe Sound's coastal fjord before turning inland at Squamish to climb through the Coast Mountains to Whistler Village. The first half is coastal and rolling — dramatic ocean-and-mountain views with punchy climbs at Furry Creek and Britannia Beach. The second half is the grind — a sustained ascent from Squamish through the Cheakamus Valley and Garibaldi Highlands, where the ocean recedes and the mountains close in. The highway is fully closed to traffic, giving riders a car-free lane on pavement that was resurfaced for the 2010 Winter Olympics. The finish in Whistler Village at 670 meters elevation feels earned — you started at the ocean and climbed through the clouds to get here.

suffering_zones.label: Stanley Park Start & Lions Gate Bridge
suffering_zones.desc: Rolling out from Stanley Park in the pre-dawn light, 5,000 riders funnel onto the Lions Gate Bridge. The suspension bridge sways imperceptibly underfoot. The sun rises over the North Shore mountains. Burrard Inlet glitters below. This is the most photographed moment of the day, and it happens before you've burned 50 calories. Enjoy it — the highway awaits.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Flat urban roads, bridge crossing, neutral rollout

suffering_zones.label: Horseshoe Bay to Howe Sound
suffering_zones.desc: Through West Vancouver and past Horseshoe Bay, the Sea-to-Sky Highway begins in earnest. Howe Sound opens up on your left — a glacially carved fjord with islands, mountain walls, and water that shifts from grey to turquoise depending on the light. The road is rolling with a gentle upward trend. This is the easy money — spectacular scenery on fast, smooth pavement with minimal climbing. Don't be fooled. The highway takes but it gives first.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Rolling coastal highway, gentle grade, ocean views

suffering_zones.label: Furry Creek & Britannia Beach Climbs
suffering_zones.desc: The first real test. The Furry Creek climb is 1.67 km at 5.8% average — a punch that arrives just as the legs are settling into a rhythm. Britannia Beach follows with a similar effort. These aren't long by alpine standards, but they're steep enough to break a draft group and separate riders who overcooked the first 45 km. The views of Howe Sound from the top of these pitches are your reward. Aid station at Britannia Beach — take the water, you'll need it later.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Punchy climbs at 5-6% average, 1-2 km each, coastal backdrop

suffering_zones.label: Squamish — The False Halfway
suffering_zones.desc: The Stawamus Chief granite monolith announces Squamish like a geological exclamation point. You're roughly halfway in distance but barely a third of the way through the climbing. This is where the ocean says goodbye and the mountains say hello. The road turns inland. The grade tilts up. The sustained climbing that defines the second half of this ride begins here. If you're not fueled and hydrated by Squamish, the next 40 miles will teach you a lesson about preparation.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Transition from coastal to mountain terrain, beginning of sustained climb

suffering_zones.label: The Cheakamus Grind
suffering_zones.desc: From Squamish northward, the Sea-to-Sky Highway earns its name. The road climbs steadily through the Cheakamus Valley and past Brandywine Falls. The gradient is never savage — mostly 2-4% — but it's relentless. No flat sections to recover. No descents to coast. Just a sustained uphill grind through increasingly mountainous terrain. Afternoon headwinds can funnel down the valley. Heat radiates off the pavement. This is where undertrained riders discover the difference between wanting to ride to Whistler and being ready to ride to Whistler.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Sustained 2-4% grade, valley headwinds, no recovery sections

suffering_zones.label: Garibaldi Highlands to Whistler Village
suffering_zones.desc: The final push through the Garibaldi Highlands and Function Junction into Whistler. The grade eases slightly as you approach the village, but tired legs don't notice subtlety. The forest opens up. Whistler's ski runs appear on the mountains above. The last few kilometers flatten into the village, and the finish-line festival — music, cheering, cold beer — pulls you forward. You started at the ocean. You climbed through the mountains. You're in Whistler. The highway was yours.
suffering_zones.terrain_detail: Moderate climbing easing to flat approach into Whistler Village

course_description.signature_challenge: The second half — from Squamish to Whistler. The coastal scenery fades, the sustained climbing begins, and the relentless 2-4% grade through the Cheakamus Valley separates the trained from the hopeful. Most of the 2,300m of climbing loads into the final 60 km.

guide_variables.race_location: Vancouver to Whistler, British Columbia, Canada
guide_variables.race_terrain: Fully paved Sea-to-Sky Highway — coastal rolling then sustained mountain climbing
guide_variables.race_weather: Mild late summer: 50-75F (10-24C), generally dry but rain possible
race_challenges: 2,300m of climbing with the majority loaded into the second half after Squamish
Sustained 2-4% grade through the Cheakamus Valley — relentless, not steep
Possible afternoon headwinds in the Squamish-to-Whistler corridor
Temperature differential between cool sea-level start and warmer inland valleys
Point-to-point nutrition strategy — 4 aid stations over 122 km requires self-sufficiency between stops

non_negotiables.requirement: Build endurance for sustained climbing in the second half
non_negotiables.why: The first 60 km along Howe Sound are rolling and scenic — deceptively comfortable. The second 60 km from Squamish to Whistler are a sustained grind at 2-4% with most of the 2,300m of climbing concentrated here. Complete at least two training rides of 100+ km with 1,500+ meters of climbing. Train your legs for hours of low-grade climbing, not short steep efforts. If you can ride 4 hours uphill at tempo, you can ride the Sea-to-Sky.

non_negotiables.requirement: Layering strategy for the sea-to-mountain temperature transition
non_negotiables.why: You start at sea level in Vancouver at 6-7 AM when it's 50-55F and potentially foggy. By the time you reach Squamish, the sun is up and the temperature has risen 15-20 degrees. A packable vest for the Lions Gate Bridge crossing and early Howe Sound sections is essential. Arm warmers that come off after the first 30 km. Don't overdress for the start — you'll overheat by Britannia Beach.

non_negotiables.requirement: Nutrition plan for 3.5-8.5 hours of sustained effort
non_negotiables.why: Aid stations are spaced every 25 km, but that's 45-75 minutes between feeds depending on your pace. Carry enough calories and fluids to be self-sufficient between stops. The second half climbing burns more calories than the flat first half — increase intake after Squamish, not before. Practice your nutrition strategy on long training rides. Many riders bonk in the Cheakamus Valley because they fueled for the first half, not the second.

Whistler Village hotels fill up months before GranFondo weekend. Vancouver is easier but still competitive. If you want to stay in Whistler Saturday night to enjoy the finish festival, book early or pay premium prices. Squamish is a budget-friendly middle ground. The return bus to Vancouver is included but doesn't leave until the afternoon.

videos.title: RBC GranFondo Whistler Official Video
videos.description:
Speedboats, drone, multiple Helijet helicopters, camera cars: the making of the 2018 RBC GranFondo Whistler event video had it all. Follow our hero Bejan as he takes on North America's best voted Gran Fondo from start to finish.

Learn more and register now: http://on.rbcgranfondo.com/trk/learn

Created by Alterna Films in association with Tourism Whistler, Resort Municipality of Whistler, Tourism Vancouver, Destination BC and RBC GranFondo Whistler. With thanks to the Province of British Columbia.

videos.title: RBC Gran Fondo 2023, Vancouver to Whistler 122km, 2017 m elevation gain.
videos.description:
Vancouver to Whistler. Spetember 9, 2023

Relive video - https://www.relive.cc/view/vMv8gEwQjNq

videos.title: 2025 RBC Grand Fondo Vancouver to Whistler
RBC GranFondo Whistler 15 years: One year for one day.
videos.description:
Get an inside look at how the RBC GranFondo Whistler comes together, from both the event organizers and riders' perspectives.

Don’t miss out on the adventure—register now for the 2025 ride on September 6th!

Register here:   https://on.rbcgranfondo.com/trk/registerweb

can become a little bit more blunt yeah
so I want to make sure that every one
understands that when we communicate we
communicate with respect we communicate
with
efficiency you know the rule of thumb is
if you're having fun probably the people
around you are having fun and let's keep
the momentum moving we work very closely
with our partners at Miller capano
wonderful people that are you know super
enthusiastic about the event our biggest
cost by far is putting down 10,000
pylons on an open and dedicated Highway
for 122 km so we actually have to
re-engineer the highway so that we can
always have traffic for for for three
groups a Northbound traffic southbound
traffic and cyclist uh Grand fond
Whistler has been voted the best Grand
Fondo in North America in 2018 and 2019
and is celebrating its 15th ride this
year on September the 7th now therefore
I can send the mayor of the city of
Vancouver on behalf of all of us the
Vancouver uh you hereby proclaimed
September 7th 2024 as Grand Fondo day in
the city
of the ShakeOut ride before the event
the Sunday slowdown and all those other
activities um they take a surprising
amount of time and effort to plan as
well and um we just want to make sure
that everyone if you're committing to
coming to this event um that you have
the opportunity to turn into a whole
week long kind of activity and and kind
of make it worth your time and effort I
just think that the most important
people at this event the writers are
obviously the most obvious but the
volunteers are huge we could not do this
without them there's over 600 people
through Thursday Friday Saturday even
Sunday and often times they're doing
things like getting up at St o00 in the

great day hey we're here I'm here to

nice the white is very sharp and I'm

there's lots of samples to try lots of

different products okay this is the part

massages okay see you

at about 3:30 4: a.m. in the morning and

in the morning um definitely a little
nervous um but now I'm here like relaxed
and just ready to take it all in
whatever it might come and uh yeah it'll
be an adventure I know it's a really
really big challenge um and I'm really
glad that you took this on with us um I
am very very proud of every person that

intended um and I hope hope you have the

videos.title: Whistler Gran Fondo 2024 [FULL]
videos.description:
GoPro recording of the 2024 Whistler Gran Fondo (122km). Official time: 3:54:55
---
8:45 Lions Gate Bridge
13:25 Taylor Way Climb
16:30 Highway 1 to Horseshoe Bay
32:50 The Bridge of Many Flats
35:00 Horseshoe Bay / Rest Stop #1
50:05 Lions Bay
57:50 Descent to Brunswick Beach
01:08:30 Porteau Cove / Rest Stop #2
01:16:00 Furry Creek Climb
01:21:10 Descent to Britannia Beach
01:31:58 Rolling through a cone
01:33:50 Descent to Squamish
01:37:30 Stawamus Chief
01:37:45 Squamish Obstacle Course
01:56:32 Suicidal photographer
01:56:20 Alice Lake climb
02:05:10 Rest Stop #3
02:06:50 Saying goodbye to the front group
02:15:55 Cheakamus climb
02:22:00 Tantalus lookout descent
02:37:10 Caught by the 2nd big group
02:41:52 Rest Stop #4
02:44:10 Windy descent
02:58:50 Caught by the 3rd big group
03:19:10 Brandywine Falls / Rest Stop #5
03:44:30 Arriving to Whistler area
03:54:00 Whistler Village
03:54:20 Sprint to the finish line
videos.curation_reason: Complete first-person race ride-along with excellent view count and detailed course markers including key climbs and landmarks.

key_challenges.name: Whistler logistics setup
key_challenges.description: Event organizers have to be in and out pretty quickly in Whistler so they don't have a whole lot of time to have the entire thing set up, execute the operation and then pull it all down basically the same day of the event

additional_quotes.text: It really is a full-time job because basically we started work the day after the grand Fondo is finished for the next year

Throughout the winter and through the spring I've had one or two rides in a week usually what that looks like is one kind of shorter ride in the middle of the week so 50 to 80k and then on the weekend it's usually over 100 km with friends

We pride ourselves about being about participation and doing the journey however long it takes and everybody gets the same experience if you're cardio or if you're athletic enough to do it faster than it's great but we really care about the never ridden just started

rider_intel.search_text: The RBC GranFondo Whistler is described as an inclusive participation-focused event that welcomes riders of all abilities, from experienced cyclists to those who have never ridden or just started. The event requires extensive year-round planning with organizers working full-time starting the day after each event to prepare for the next year. Logistically, the event presents challenges as organizers must set up, execute, and tear down operations in Whistler on the same day due to time constraints. Participants describe taking time to train and enjoy the views while pushing themselves to feel accomplished. The event emphasizes participation over speed, ensuring all riders get the same experience regardless of their fitness level or completion time. Training approaches mentioned include one to two rides per week with shorter midweek rides of 50-80km and longer weekend rides over 100km with friends.
